data.name: Luis Mejía
data.description: Given the nickname Manotas (Mitts) because of his big hands, Mejía made his Primera División for Tauro aged 16 before moving to sign for Fénix in Uruguay. After a career path that has also gone via Chile and, very briefly, France, the goalkeeper returned to Uruguay with Nacional in 2024, where he has won plenty of silverware. Something of a veteran in the international setup having earned his first cap in 2009, the 31-year-old has rarely been first-choice at a major tournament and missed out on the squad for the 2018 World Cup all together. He is the backup goalkeeper behind Orlando Mosquera in North America this summer. “We have to demonstrate that hierarchy and that experience,” he has said of the senior figures in the Panama squad.

data.name: César Blackman
data.description: At the age of 19, Blackman left Panama behind to move to Slovakia. He was already a league champion with El Chorrillo, having made his debut aged 17 before securing a transfer to Dunajska Streda, following in the footsteps of his compatriot Eric Davis. He had to adapt to a new country, language and way of life in Europe. His attacking qualities and pace when defending one-on-one attracted the attention of Slovan Bratislava in 2023. He is now an established starter at Slovakia’s biggest club, having won two league titles, and has become the first Panamanian to play in the Champions League. He celebrates each goal by pointing to a heart tattoo on his arm – a tribute to his parents, who died when he was less than five years old. “How I wished my mum and dad were here,” he said, wearing a T-shirt with a picture of them on, after Panama secured qualification for this World Cup.

data.name: José Córdoba
data.description: Córdoba’s European adventure began at the age of 19 with a move to Celta Vigo, where he played for the reserves. The defender made his name with Etar in Bulgaria and earned a move to Levski Sofia, winning the 2024 Bulgarian Cup. Norwich came calling that year and the left-footed centre-back has become a mainstay with the Championship side. “Today is a day to be happy. This is a childhood dream and I’m excited for this next step in my career,” he said upon his arrival in England. Córdoba is elegant with the ball at his feet, a threat in the air and cuts out opposition attacks at source. The Prince of Panama, as he’s known at Carrow Road, already has more than 30 caps.

data.name: Fidel Escobar
data.description: Escobar was still having kickabouts on the street in his home neighbourhood of El Chorrillo in Panama City when he made his first-team debut for San Francisco aged 17. “El Comendante” is now an imposing figure at the back and a leader. After breaking through at New York Red Bulls alongside compatriot Amir Murillo, he had spells in Spain’s lower leagues before a move to Saprissa in 2022. The defender has become an idol in Costa Rica, winning four league titles and scoring in the Torneo de Copa final victory in April this year. When he won his first trophy with the club, he looked to the sky and dedicated it to his late mother. “This is what she wanted,” he said. After overcoming a hernia problem, he will play at his second World Cup, after 2018.

data.name: Edgardo Fariña
data.description: Fariña stands 1.93m (6ft 4in) tall and has been called the Gigantic Panamanian, making him a useful player to have in both boxes. He came through the academies of Río Abajo and Plaza Amador before making his senior debut with Atlético Nacional in Panama’s second tier. Impressive spells at Independiente and Universitario led to a move to Municipal in Guatemala, where his defensive solidity helped them win the Clausura title in 2024. “You feel like Municipal is a very united family,” he said on his arrival. Dominant in the air and a good reader of the play, the 24-year-old landed a move to Russia with Khimki in 2024 before joining Nizhny Novgorod a year later. The centre-back was part of the Panama team that won the Maurice Revello Under-23 trophy in 2023.

data.name: Cristian Martínez
data.description: A combative tackler and effective attacker, Martínez is your typical box-to-box midfielder. He is from El Chorrillo in Panama City, making his debut for the local side as a teenager. His dynamism and stamina earned him a move to Columbus Crew in MLS – where he became the club’s youngest scorer – before joining Cádiz in Spain and having other spells in Venezuela, Saudi Arabia and back in Panama. The 2023 Gold Cup runner-up signed for Kiryat Shmona in Israel last year and was a key part of the successful World Cup qualification campaign. “My childhood dream was to wear the national team’s shirt and put my soul into each game,” he says. Nicknamed El Fulo – the Blond One – for his tendency to dye his hair bleach-blond.

data.name: José Luis Rodríguez
data.description: Rodríguez lost his father at the age of 10 but the youngster from Panama City found the strength to dream big through football and his family – his brother Marcos is a former captain of El Chorrillo. A winger with lightning speed, “El Puma” has made a name for himself in Belgium, Spain, Portugal and Serbia. He isn’t afraid to try his luck from distance either, scoring via a deflection off Tunisia’s Yassine Meriah in the group stage of the 2018 World Cup. The 27-year-old, now playing in Mexico, has found a mentor in the Panama head coach, Thomas Christiansen. “I’m very grateful to ‘El Profe’ … we know each other very well and we’re like a family,” Rodríguez has said. His mother still celebrates every goal with tears of pride in her eyes.

data.name: Adalberto Carrasquilla
data.description: “Coco” was running his own business aged 16 when he made his first-team debut for Tauro, cutting his teammates’ hair for a few dollars in order to help out his family. This graft and humility have taken him far. The 27-year-old is the driving force of Panama’s midfield with his tireless running, good feet and great vision. He works hard to recover possession but is also known for taking up dangerous positions in the final third. After winning two league titles with Tauro, he was a US Open Cup winner with Houston Dynamo in 2023 before signing for Pumas in Mexico for a club-record fee in January 2025. At the 2023 Gold Cup, when Panama were runners-up to Mexico, Carrasquilla won the Golden Ball for best player at the tournament. Then he was key to World Cup qualification. “It was our dream,” he said. Whenever he returns to Panama, he still gets his scissors out to cut his friends’ hair.

data.name: Tomás Rodríguez
data.description: “It’s a big club and it wins things,” said Rodríguez upon his arrival at Saprissa in Costa Rica last January. He had earned the big move after his performances for Monagas in Venezuela, where he became the club’s top foreign goalscorer in just two years. But Rodríguez struggled to adapt to the pressure of Saprissa. “I was very miserable, very depressed,” he said. The striker wears his heart on his sleeve and is often emotional after he finds the net. “TomyGol” has now started to find his feet in Costa Rica, and Panama will hope he can reproduce the form he showed at last year’s Gold Cup, scoring in each of the group games at his first major tournament.

data.name: Ismael Díaz
data.description: Díaz made his first-team debut for Tauro aged 15 and attempted to launch his career in Europe at Porto. Injury problems hampered his progression in the B team but he came back stronger, emerging as a goalscoring machine with Universidad Católica in Ecuador. After 55 goals in three seasons made him a star of the Liga Pro, the winger landed a move to Club León in Mexico last year. The 29-year-old has explosive speed and is a lethal finisher, having scored a hat-trick for Panama during their run to the 2023 Gold Cup final. Nicknamed El Príncipe (the Prince) since childhood, Díaz is one of Panama’s greatest hopes of upsetting the applecart at this World Cup. Asked by Fifa.com why Panama play with so much passion, Díaz replied: “A lot of us come from the streets, we come from humble backgrounds, where maybe we had enough to be able to play in football schools, which I remember really well.“

data.name: Yoel Bárcenas
data.description: El Mago earned his nickname owing to his outstanding technical ability with the ball at his feet. The mazy dribbler made his senior debut for Árabe Unido, his boyhood club, and won multiple league titles before an initial move to Europe with Croatia’s RNK Split. Having won promotion to Mexico’s top flight with Cafetaleros in 2018, Bárcenas got a second shot at Europe with Real Oviedo. He shone in Spain, also playing for Girona and Leganés over a number of years in the second tier. The 32-year-old has become one of Panamanian football’s leading lights – three of his 100+ caps came at the 2018 World Cup and he was named in the team of the tournament at the 2023 Gold Cup. “I want to be more important for Panama and make history at the 2026 World Cup,” he says.

data.name: César Samudio
data.description: The 32-year-old has been canonised by supporters of Marathón in Honduras – they call him Saint Samudio for his heroics in goal. He made his senior league debut in Panama for Chepo aged 20 and went on to play for a series of modest clubs such as Atlético Veragüense, Costa del Este and Plaza Amador before winning the title with Independiente in 2022, when he was named player of the season and kept the most clean sheets. He is now an idol in Honduras, where he has set records for clean sheets, and says he has “fallen in love with these colours” at Marathón. He is a backup option for Thomas Christiansen, the Panama coach, this summer.

data.name: Jiovany Ramos
data.description: “I will play every match as if it were a final,” Ramos said in 2022, when he signed for Deportivo Táchira – then the third Venezuelan club of his career. The fiercely competitive centre-back lifted the Liga FutVe the following year and earned a move to Alianza Lima, becoming one of only a few Panamanians to play for the Peruvian giants. A return to Venezuela followed and the defender now turns out for Academia Puerto Cabello. Ramos won the Panamanian Clausura with Independiente in 2020 and has built a reputation as a born leader while representing the national team at the 2023 Gold Cup and in the Concacaf Nations League.

data.name: Carlos Harvey
data.description: Harvey’s American dream began in Los Angeles, where he secured a loan move from Panama’s Tauro to LA Galaxy’s second team. His performances for Galaxy II were enough to earn a permanent move and a first-team contract but it was at Phoenix Rising where he made his breakthrough, winning the United Soccer League Championship in 2023. A defensive midfielder, who can also play at centre-back, the 26-year-old now calls James Rodríguez his teammate at Minnesota and has renewed his contract with the MLS club until 2028. “I’ve grown a lot as a player and a person,” he said upon signing his new deal. Harvey will be hoping his mix of tenacity and tactical intelligence can impress at his first World Cup.

data.name: Eric Davis
data.description: The veteran defender was given a guard of honour by his teammates at Dunajska Streda at his final game for the Slovakian club in 2023. They were his first port of call in Europe and he stayed for eight years, racking up 200 games, 20 goals and 32 assists. Davis had a big reputation even before leaving Panama, winning two league titles with Árabe Unido after making his debut aged 18. An accomplished crosser of the ball, the left-back has now returned to Panama after spells with MLS’s DC United, Slovakia’s Kosice and Brazil’s Vila Nova. “Returning to my country is something very special,” he said after signing for Plaza Amador. The 35-year-old has more than 100 caps for the national team and travels to his second World Cup.

data.name: Andrés Andrade
data.description: Andrade became the first player from Panama to feature in the German Bundesliga when at Arminia Bielefeld but it is in the Austrian Bundesliga where he has established himself in European football. He arrived in Linz at Lask as a 19-year-old in 2018 and, after that spell in Germany, returned in 2023 as a regular starter. The defender’s ability to establish himself in Austria is even more impressive when you consider he had a difficult time as a teenager with Querétaro and Toluca in Mexico. His perseverance has yielded rewards and this will be his first World Cup. The 2023 Gold Cup runner-up says: “it’s always a proud moment to be able to represent Panama.”

data.name: José Fajardo
data.description: Fajardo knows what it’s like to star in North America. During the 2024 Copa América he netted vital goals against the USA – the hosts – and Bolivia that earned Panama a place in the knockout stages. The former DC United striker also scored crucial goals in Panama’s World Cup qualification campaign, adding to his national hero status. But Fajardo did not play a game of senior football until the age of 24, working jobs on the Panama Canal to support his young family instead. “Life is not easy, you have to suffer. I’m motivated to keep going with my football and be an example to children and the people from my hometown,” he said. He soon proved his worth for Independiente de La Chorrera, his goals helping to propel the team to two league titles. After spells in Peru, MLS and Saudi Arabia, the 32-year-old is now playing in Ecuador.

data.name: Cecilio Waterman
data.description: When Waterman scored a stoppage-time winner to send Panama into the 2025 Nations League final at the United States’ expense, he jumped on top of the pitchside hoardings and spotted Thierry Henry doing TV punditry. “You’re my idol!” Waterman shouted at him before the whole Panama squad jumped on the World Cup winner in celebration. The 35-year-old is nicknamed Aquaman, for obvious reasons, and his superpower is scoring goals. He has an envious record for a number of clubs in Chile and lifted the league title for the first time with Coquimbo Unido in 2025. He counts Cobresal and Everton among his previous employers, as well as Fénix in Uruguay, and now leads the line for Universidad de Concepción.

data.name: Alberto Quintero
data.description: Quintero made his professional debut two decades ago with El Chorrillo in Panama City. He comes from the humble neighbourhood of the same name and pursued a career in football as a way of providing for his family. “My main strength is perseverance, 100%,” he has said. Gifted and skilful but also tireless and versatile, Quintero became a modern winger with his ability to go past players and set up opponents. His 20-year career has taken him to Spain, Colombia, Mexico and the United States, while he is an iconic figure at Universitario de Deportes in Peru. He returned to Panama two years ago to finish his career with Plaza Amador. He played a key role in Panama’s historic qualification for the 2018 World Cup but broke his foot in a warm-up match before heading to Russia. After over 140 caps for his country – and just a couple since 2023 – the 38-year-old can make his World Cup bow.

data.name: Aníbal Godoy
data.description: A selección stalwart for 16 years and now the captain, Godoy is the leading figure of this Panama squad. He has over 150 caps, three of which came at the 2018 World Cup, and has played in two Gold Cup finals. “Each time I play for Panama, it’s like touching the sky,” he has said. Two decades ago Godoy made his league debut as a teenager for Chepo and immediately became a regular in midfield. His big break with Godoy Cruz in Argentina was followed by a spell in Hungary before his arrival in MLS. After a few years apiece at San Jose Earthquakes and Nashville, he is one of the leaders at San Diego, one of the league’s newest franchises. The left-footed veteran is now a naturalised citizen of the United States.

data.name: César Yanis
data.description: One of many to come out of Panama City’s El Chorrillo neighbourhood, Yanis spent his childhood playing football with friends. “We played until it went dark and we dreamed big,” he says. Nicknamed Yanisinho for his dribbling skills akin to a Brazilian's, the winger took his first steps with Potros del Este, scoring decisive goals in promotion campaigns and winning league and cup titles. After adventures in Spain and Costa Rica, he has found a home in Chile with Cobresal. With Panama he is a 2023 Gold Cup runner-up and took just 33 seconds after coming off the bench to score the third goal in a 3-1 win over Bolivia that secured Los Canaleros’ passage into the knockouts. “We were born to make history,” he said then. A family man, Yanis once flew home from Chile to surprise his mum on Mother’s Day.

data.name: Orlando Mosquera
data.description: Mosquera is up against some of football’s greatest goalscorers each week in the Saudi Pro League but he himself actually knows what it’s like to find the net. While a young goalkeeper at Tauro in 2015, he scored a last-minute equaliser as his team chased the league title but then got injured. “It’s not how you start, it’s how you finish,” he told his coach. “I’ll recover and we’ll be champions.” He left Tauro as a three-time Primera División winner and spent time in Turkey, Bolivia, Venezuela and Israel before arriving at Al-Fayha, where he plays behind Chris Smalling. “Kuty” is a commanding presence between the posts and takes charge of his six-yard box. He was Panama’s first-choice for the 2023 Gold Cup run and is appearing at his first World Cup.

data.name: Michael Amir Murillo
data.description: Murillo made his senior league debut in Panama for San Francisco in 2014 after standing out in a youth trial that included more than 130 players. He grew up in Colón, sharing a room with his mother and siblings, but while baseball was the family’s favourite sport, he chose football and wanted to provide. “My main strength is my mentality, 100%,” he has said. His pace, crossing ability and aerial prowess make him a modern full-back. Murillo’s big break came in MLS with New York Red Bulls, where he was the first player from Panama to feature in an All-Star game and won the 2018 Supporters’ Shield. After spells with Anderlecht and Marseille, he signed for Besiktas in February. A key part of the Panama side that qualified for the 2018 World Cup and performed well at the 2023 Gold Cup, the defender is a national treasure and has never forgotten his roots. “Be proud of your family, fight for your family and believe in your family,” he always says.

data.name: Azarias Londoño
data.description: Londoño enjoyed a successful first season with Universidad Católica in 2025, on loan from Alianza Lima, scoring seven goals and providing 10 assists in the Liga Pro and lifting the Copa Ecuador. “Winning this trophy is valuable for the club, it was the goal that we were pushing for,” he said. The 24-year-old striker is good with his head, tending to win most of his aerial duels, but is also capable of holding his nerve one-on-one with the goalkeeper. As a 19-year-old, he scored twice on his first-team debut for Árabe Unido and went on to win the apertura title in 2022. After 14 goals in his first season at Comunicaciones in Guatemala, he failed to find form in Portugal’s second tier with Torreense. After two Gold Cup inclusions, this will be his first World Cup.

data.name: Roderick Miller
data.description: Miller will not be overawed by the travel schedule of this summer’s World Cup. He has played in seven different countries throughout his career and has found a home in Azerbaijan. The left-footed centre-back has become a fan favourite at Turan Tovuz with his performances in their backline. Since joining in 2023 he has impressed with his aerial prowess and his composure on the ball. The 34-year-old has had spells in Mexico, Portugal, Kazakhstan, Peru and Iraq but still has his family at heart. “I’m lucky to have a princess by my side,” he said of his daughter, Anahís, his biggest motivation. “I was a young dad and, while it’s not easy, it has been a marvellous experience.” A Panama international since 2011, Miller has over 40 caps to his name.

data.name: Jorge Gutiérrez
data.description: In Tocumen, a stone’s throw from Panama City’s airport, a boy called Jorge used to kick a ball around in an academy that did not yet exist. Club Atlético Tocumen was set up in the years that followed and Guti, as he is known, had trials with Tauro, in his words “the biggest and most successful club in Panama”, and made his Primera División debut aged 16. He won four league titles before the door to Europe opened. He was loaned to Melilla, a team in the Spanish enclave in Morocco that played in the third tier. It didn’t work out and he moved to Venezuela. Inspired by Marcelo and Dani Alves, Gutiérrez is a Brazilian full-back at heart – great at going past players and a threat in the opposition third. He is one of the best full-backs in Venezuela and competes with the veteran Eric Davis for a starting place for Panama this summer.
